content.full_text:
February 13, 1991

California Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95894-0807

Gentlemen:

Your letter of November 7, 1990 to the Mayor of the City of Folsom, California, reference FPPC No. 87-242, has raised a question in my mind concerning its possible application to the common practice of requiring a developer to pay the cost of preparation of an Environmental Impact Report for his project.

I know two ways of doing this: by a three-way contract between the developer, the public agency, and a consultant chosen by the public agency to prepare the EIR; and by two separate contracts, one between the developer and the public agency and one between the consultant and the public agency. In either case, the developer deposits the estimated cost of the EIR preparation in advance with the agency, which uses the money to pay the consultant, or in some cases, the developer reimburses the public agency after the consultant has been paid the amount set forth in the developer's contract.

Do any, all, or some of the above procedures, in your opinion, violate the Political Reform Act? Does it matter whether there is express, statutory authorization for the use of consultants for preparation of project EIR's? Does it matter that the consultant is not the ultimate decision-maker on either the project or the adequacy of the EIR, and that the public agency's staff has extensive input to, and exercises considerable control over, the content of the EIR?

February 13, 1991

Perhaps I am reading too much into your opinion letter to the City of Folsom, but I am concerned over its possible wider application and would greatly appreciate clarification as requested above.

Very truly yours,

Eleanor M. Egan,
City Attorney

cc: H. Dale Jones, Chief Administrative Officer
     Jody Hall-Esser, Community Development Director
     Evelyn Keller, Deputy City Attorney
